SEED LAUNCH MODULE 3
PRODUCT CREATION – PART 1
Welcome to Module Three. This is going to be all about your product creation and
delivery.
I know I threw a ton of stuff at you with those first two modules and I know it was intense
and complicated with crazy amounts of homework. I think this module is going to be
easier to follow, easier to crunch through, you’re going to be able to get a lot done,
you’re going to be able to come up with a great plan for your product.
Product creation is something that really stops people dead in their tracks. I am a big
fan of having your own products, I’ve had my own products right from the very
beginning. Back when I started out you couldn’t sell other people’s products, there
weren’t products to sell, there weren’t affiliate programs, you sort of had to create your
own products. I was forced into that right from the start. Lo and behold, that’s one of the
real keys to being successful online is to have your own products.
For almost everyone, you’re going to want to also sell other people’s products as an
affiliate, because the reality is that you will attract people that are really very rabid about
your topic, they want more and more information. I’m that way about many topics. You
wouldn’t believe how many mountain bike magazines I have sitting around the house,
it’s just crazy, or how many products I have about playing guitar. We don’t even want to
get into business and marketing products, I actually have a storage facility just for all my
extra training products that I’ve bought.
When you have someone on your list they will want to buy a lot of stuff and you will
never be able to satisfy all of their needs, so it’s great to have additional products to sell
via affiliate programs. However, you really need to have your own products because
that way you control your own destiny. No one can take those away.
If you’re selling an affiliate product and all of a sudden the product is not as good or the
customer service goes downhill, then you’re going to have to stop promoting that. But if
you have your own product and it’s a great product, then you are in control of your own
destiny, you can get other people to promote you as an affiliate. There are just lots of
reasons why you want your own products. Obviously you’re already sold on that idea,
otherwise you wouldn’t be on this call.
The thing is, sometimes creating your own products can really just stop you dead in the
water because it just feels like such a crazy insurmountable thing to do. I’m going to

Interaction is Your Friend…
Feedback is absolutely the lifeblood of creating a great product. We all suffer this thing
called the curse of knowledge. I read about this in the book Made to Stick by Chip and
Dan Heath. The curse of knowledge is the idea of once we become experts in
something it’s almost impossible for us to remember when we were not experts and we
forget all the simple questions and what people are like when in that beginner mind.
We often create a product that ends up being way over people’s heads. When you base
your product on feedback, on interaction, then all you have to do is open up your ears
and you can remember what it’s like to be in that beginner mind because people are
going to be asking you all those questions.
The other thing is it’s just really easy to answer questions. It’s a lot easier to answer 10
questions versus trying to come up with an hour long lecture. It’s way easier to answer
questions every day of the week. It makes your products better because you avoid that
curse of knowledge.
This is how you get the feedback. Again, this course is all about you modeling me, so

The thing with teleseminars is basically it’s a service. You sign up for the service and

identification code. One PIN will be for you as the presenter and one is for all the
attendees.
Whoever has bought your product, you send them the link for the attendees and you tell
them what time to call in. You give them the number, the PIN, and time, and they just
call in. You call the same number but you enter your PIN, the presenter’s PIN. Then all
you do is talk on the phone and everyone can hear you. It’s that simple.
There’s only one piece that’s different from making a phone call and that’s when you
enter your PIN number. Since all of you are on this call, you’re all obviously capable of
using technology and at least entering a PIN.

How to structure each call – this is a little bit conceptual, but this is important.

did a great job with it. This is something that I’ve been studying and the work goes way
back way before Simon ever did that video. The thing is you have to tell them why.
The reason is that there’s about 34% of the population out there that if they don’t know
their why their brains just turn off and they can’t go any further. You can go really deep
into this topic. That 34% of folks, if you don’t tell them the why, they can’t listen to any of
the what or the how to. Interestingly enough, those people tend to be more sales type of
people. Often those folks are in some type of sales function in their profession.
Next, you have to cover the what. This is a little different than what Simon teaches in
that video. It’s a good video, but it’s not the be all and end all.
The what is the next thing to cover after the why. What are you actually talking about?

This is important – these next two points are a couple of key important points.
When you create a survey you want to keep it as short as possible. You want to look at
it like every single question asked will lower the response. You want to make it so that
people can just get in and out. If they show up for a survey and there are 12 questions,
a lot of them just aren’t even going to begin it. Always keep it as short as possible.
Make sure that every question you ask them that you ask yourself, “Do I really need to
ask this question? Is this going to give me data? Is this going to give me useful data that
I’m going to use and that I really want?” Really sweat each question and whether you
need to put it on there. If you look, the surveys I’ve been sending to you are two very
simple questions.
This next one is very important and it came from hard learned experience.
You want to use a redirect or a URL shortener, such as bit.ly or another service. The
reason is once you start the survey you can’t really change it. If you say, “This question
is a stupid question, I don’t need this question so I want to delete this question,” or, “I
want to change this question to a multiple choice instead of an essay response.” You
can’t, you actually have to go and create a new one.
In some cases you can change it and make some minor changes, but in many cases if
you want to actually change the question format, the number of questions, delete a
question, add a question, you actually have to create a second survey. If you’ve already
emailed out the link to the first survey, there’s no way you can all of a sudden have it go
to a second survey.
But if you use a redirect – I don’t know what the link was for the last survey, but it was
probably sixandseven.com/seedlaunch3. When you click on that it would take you to the
survey. If I wanted to change the survey, all I would have to do is change what link that
redirect was going to.
Does that make sense, is it clear why you want to use a redirect?
This is important stuff that everyone screws up. They basically screw it up until they

When you’re putting together your survey, you want to start people with a softball

them into the survey.
If I’m doing just a broad survey about something else, I’ll often make the first question a
yes or no question, or a real, easy multiple question. They take that first question and
then they hit the answer button and it takes them to another page. That other page
might have a more involved question and then that takes them to another page. You
sort of draw them in.

sequences. Everything I do is a sequence. How do you draw people in? How do you
capture their attention and hold their attention? When I do surveys it’s the same way. I
give them an easy one and then I gradually pull them in.
Although, sometimes you want to do the opposite and repel as many people as

we have to go through each survey by hand, so I want to eliminate the tire kickers.

phone number, including every piece of contact information, and I’ll make it all required.

What’s the difference between a Kindle edition book and a PDF ebook and a hard copy
